messages: I'm trying to get better at cooking and want practical suggestions — a few specifics about my setup so advice fits: I own a 25 cm carbon-steel skillet (bought 2017-11-05), a 2.8 L enameled Dutch oven, and a 1.2 L stainless saucepan.

Appliance constraints: I have a two-burner electric coil top — one burner rated 1500 W and the other 900 W — and a compact countertop convection oven that tops out at 230°C and fits a 9x13-inch dish. The kitchen is on a single 15 A circuit, so I can't reliably run the oven and the hotter burner at full power together.
Timing: on weeknights I have exactly 40 minutes to cook starting at 6:30 PM; on weekends I can spend about 90 minutes.

Diet and pantry details that affect recipes: I'm pescatarian and allergic to shellfish, and I avoid dairy after 7:00 PM. In the pantry right now I have three sealed 150 g jars of preserved lemon slices, two unopened 400 g cans of chickpeas, a 250 g bag of fine semolina, and a 250 g block of unsalted butter (I don't use it in the evening).

Skill level: I can julienne a carrot in roughly 70 seconds and I can hold a steady medium-high sear for around four minutes per side on a protein. I'm comfortable with basic knife work but want to improve timing and coordination.
How often should I hone my chef's knife versus sharpening it on a stone if I cook three to four times a week?
When bakers talk about autolyse and stretch-and-fold, what's actually happening to the dough and how does it influence the final crumb?
Any palate-training exercises I can do to better pick out acidity, bitterness, and umami when tasting components?
Tempering chocolate intimidates me—are there beginner-friendly approaches that work without professional gear or highly specialized thermometers?
Is a handheld torch genuinely useful at home, or are there simpler ways to get a caramelized finish that still look nice?
Do you have any mnemonics or short routines that help keep mise en place efficient so I'm not frantically chopping while food cooks?
Why does salt change bread dough behavior so much — what does it do to gluten development and crust formation?
What are small prep tricks to reduce bitterness in vegetables like eggplant or radicchio?
Any practical tips for photographing plated food with natural light and just a smartphone?
What are measurable, achievable monthly goals for improving knife speed and accuracy?
Sometimes fried snacks feel greasy in the mouth—what are common causes beyond using too much oil?
Is it more effective to study general technique (why things work) or to repeat many recipes until they become second nature?
How should I think about timing when adding herbs—what belongs at the start versus right at the end?
Are stand mixers essential for home bakers, or can hand mixing and good technique produce similar results for most bakes?
What actually happens when meat rests after cooking that makes it juicier if you wait a bit before slicing?
Any short daily drills I can do to practice consistent dice sizes and improve chopping rhythm?
If a sauce tastes a bit flat, what's the quickest tasting-adjustment sequence to get it balanced (salt, acid, sweet, fat—what order)?
Which spices or aromatics do home cooks tend to underuse but can really elevate weeknight cooking?
When attempting to recreate a restaurant dish, how do you break it into components and prioritize which parts to practice first?
How can I tell if a recipe's published times are realistic for a home cook versus optimistic 'restaurant speed'?
Which affordable tools give the best boost in consistency and speed—instant-read thermometer, digital scale, immersion blender, or something else?
Any good rules of thumb for scaling recipes up or down without ruining texture or balance?
What quick techniques brighten or deepen vegetable flavor without relying on long braises or heavy creams?
My emulsified dressings sometimes break—what are the main causes and quick fixes to re-emulsify?
Which classic cookbooks focus most on technique and the 'why' behind methods rather than purely recipes?
Do you have exercises for practicing timing so a starch and a veg finish at the same time consistently?
Should I isolate practice (knife technique one week, seasoning the next) or try to practice several fundamentals together in a real-cooking flow?

If a recipe asks to sear then finish for a short period in the oven, would converting it to sear-and-rest usually save time in my tight weeknight slot, or does the oven finish materially change the result?
I have a jarred citrus condiment and a few tins of legumes in the pantry—can those be the base of a satisfying evening meal with mostly stovetop work, or do they typically need long simmering?
Because I avoid dairy later in the evening, what are good ways to build a silky, satisfying sauce for dinner without cream or butter?

Given that my kitchen can't comfortably run more than one high-draw device at a time, should I plan weeknight menus around single-heat-source recipes or is alternating steps between appliances manageable?
If I want a quick flatbread from that coarse wheat product I have, is a rapid dry-heat approach on the cooktop preferable to trying to bake it in a compact countertop unit?
Since I can hold a steady medium-high sear for a few minutes, are there prep shortcuts or sequence swaps that make a sear-first approach reliably fit into an evening when time is limited?
Would a one-pot plan using a heavier enameled vessel generally save time and reduce steps on busy weeknights compared with juggling multiple pans and transfers?
With my usual short weekday cooking window, what's a realistic number of components to aim for (protein, starch, veg, sauce) so plating and timing stay under control?
